critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Early-spring browser feeding on fresh shoots, buds, and newly exposed ground.
critters.fact: In early April, white-tailed deer often shift from winter survival foods to tender spring growth as it greens up.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, deer are commonly seen browsing field edges, openings, and trails at dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Large forest mammal emerging into a landscape that is finally waking up.
critters.fact: By early April, more black bears may be out of dens and searching for easy food after winter dormancy.
critters.activeFact: Early spring bears often focus on low-effort foods first while the woods slowly build back toward greener meals.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Active spring predator traveling open ground, field edges, and muddy trail corridors.
critters.fact: Coyotes stay busy in early spring, hunting where thawed ground exposes prey movement and scent trails.
critters.activeFact: In early April, coyotes often patrol fields and edge habitat where rodents and rabbits are easier to track.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Solitary hunter working brush lines, grassy openings, and thawed field margins.
critters.fact: Red foxes use exposed spring ground to hunt rodents in grass, brush, and patchy cover.
critters.activeFact: As more snow disappears, red foxes often become easier to spot crossing open areas in the morning or evening.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Secretive woodland fox moving through brushy cover during the spring transition.
critters.fact: Gray foxes remain hard to spot in early spring, usually sticking to dense cover while moving between feeding and den areas.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, gray foxes may adjust activity with warming days while still favoring thick edges and timber.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Elusive predator still hunting hard through muddy spring woods and edges.
critters.fact: Bobcats stay active in early spring, using brush, timber, and transition zones to stalk rabbits and other prey.
critters.activeFact: With snow mostly fading, bobcats often travel wooded margins where prey movement is picking up.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-moving feeder still using bark, buds, and early spring browse.
critters.fact: Porcupines often keep relying on woody foods in early April before soft green vegetation fully takes over.
critters.activeFact: This stage of spring still finds porcupines feeding on bark and buds while fresher plant growth slowly expands.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Wetland builder active along opening water, softened banks, and repaired flow lines.
critters.fact: Early spring often brings increased beaver maintenance work as flowing water pressures dams, channels, and lodges.
critters.activeFact: With more water open now, fresh beaver activity often shows up as cuttings, slides, and shoreline changes.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Aquatic rodent more visible as marsh edges and shorelines fully open.
critters.fact: Muskrats become easier to spot in early April as open water expands and feeding routes along wetlands reopen.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, muskrats often cruise shorelines and open pockets looking for fresh plant material.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic hunter working creek banks, muddy margins, and thawed water edges.
critters.fact: Minks take advantage of spring thaw by hunting along open shorelines where fish, frogs, and small mammals are more exposed.
critters.activeFact: In early April, mink often patrol creek bends and pond edges where prey movement is increasing.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Forest carnivore ranging through timber as spring conditions settle in.
critters.fact: Fishers remain active in early spring, traveling wooded corridors and hunting across changing ground conditions.
critters.activeFact: As the woods soften and open, fishers often keep to cover while moving ridges, timber, and edge routes.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Aquatic predator taking full advantage of returning open water.
critters.fact: River otters become easier to notice in early spring as streams, channels, and pond edges reopen for travel and hunting.
critters.activeFact: In early April, otters may be seen using open banks and water lanes to move, fish, and play.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Tree squirrel busy with feeding, chasing, and nest activity in early spring.
critters.fact: Eastern gray squirrels are highly active in early spring as breeding activity, nest work, and food gathering continue.
critters.activeFact: Warm April days often bring extra squirrel traffic through the trees as they feed and work nest sites.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Territorial woodland squirrel guarding food stores and spring space with attitude.
critters.fact: Red squirrels stay loud in early spring as they defend middens and keep tabs on old food caches.
critters.activeFact: In early April, red squirrels often sound off from favorite perches while working cone piles and cover.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Ground squirrel now appearing more regularly as spring warmth builds.
critters.fact: Chipmunks become much easier to spot in early April as sunny days and open ground boost above-ground activity.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, chipmunks often zip around logs, rocks, and old burrow entrances on mild afternoons.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Forest hare navigating the rough switch from winter camouflage to spring ground.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares can look mismatched in early spring when pale winter fur meets bare ground and patchy cover.
critters.activeFact: As April arrives, snowshoe hares may stand out more in open spots until seasonal coat change catches up.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Common rabbit feeding along brushy openings, hedges, and early green edges.
critters.fact: Eastern cottontails stay active in early spring, browsing tender shoots and using brush piles for cover.
critters.activeFact: With more ground open now, cottontails often work yard edges, hedgerows, and field margins for food.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Nocturnal forager more active as spring nights get milder and food opens up.
critters.fact: Raccoons often increase movement in early spring as thawed ground, water edges, and easier food sources return.
critters.activeFact: In early April, raccoons may range more widely around woods, water, and human edges after dark.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Cold-sensitive marsupial benefiting from milder nights and easier spring travel.
critters.fact: Opossums become easier to notice in early spring when nighttime temperatures allow longer foraging trips.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, opossums often move through brush, yards, and edge habitat looking for easy meals.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Night forager still roaming actively during the spring breeding window.
critters.fact: Striped skunks are often on the move in early spring as breeding activity keeps them wandering on mild nights.
critters.activeFact: Early April can still mean extra skunk movement around open ground, fields, and damp edge habitat.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Gliding nocturnal squirrel staying active through cool but improving spring nights.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels remain active in early spring, gliding between trees to feed on buds, seeds, and stored foods.
critters.activeFact: Even now, flying squirrels stay mostly hidden by day while quietly working the treetops after dark.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Insect-eating bat beginning to benefit from warmer nights and rising bug activity.
critters.fact: Little brown bats may become more active in early April as flying insects start increasing on mild evenings.
critters.activeFact: A soft spring night near water can bring little brown bats out for one of the season’s early feeding runs.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Hardy bat ready to use early spring warm spells near lights and buildings.
critters.fact: Big brown bats can become active earlier than many bats when early spring evenings warm enough for insect flight.
critters.activeFact: In early April, big brown bats may show up on warmer nights around structures, lights, and open edges.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Woodland owl still vocal as spring settles into the hardwoods.
critters.fact: Barred owls remain very vocal in early spring as pair bonds and territory activity stay strong.
critters.activeFact: Early April is still a great time to hear barred owls calling through the woods after sunset or before dawn.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Powerful owl often already deep into nesting while spring gets started.
critters.fact: Great horned owls are among the earliest nesters and may already be raising young by early April.
critters.activeFact: By now, great horned owls can be well into family duties even while nights still feel cold.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Bold songbird louder and more territorial as nesting season gets serious.
critters.fact: Blue jays become increasingly active in early April as pair bonds strengthen and nest scouting ramps up.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, blue jays often call hard, chase rivals, and treat feeders like claimed property.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Bright red songbird fully engaged in spring singing and territory season.
critters.fact: Northern cardinals sing heavily in early April as males defend territory and attract mates.
critters.activeFact: A cardinal singing from a high perch is one of the clearest sounds of early spring settling in.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Energetic feeder bird moving deeper into pair-bonding and cavity-checking season.
critters.fact: Black-capped chickadees become more vocal in early spring as they strengthen pair bonds and inspect nesting spots.
critters.activeFact: In early April, chickadees often balance quick feeding with more calling, chasing, and pair behavior.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious woodland songbird growing louder and more territorial in early spring.
critters.fact: Tufted titmice increase calling and territory behavior in early April as cavity checking and nesting prep continue.
critters.activeFact: This stage of spring often brings titmice to exposed perches where they call across the woods.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Tree-climbing bird active on bark, trunks, and spring nest routes.
critters.fact: White-breasted nuthatches stay busy in early spring, foraging along trunks and checking likely nest cavities.
critters.activeFact: In early April, nuthatches often get more vocal while still hammering seeds into bark cracks.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small woodpecker drumming steadily as spring territory season strengthens.
critters.fact: Downy woodpeckers increase drumming in early spring to advertise territory and prepare for nesting.
critters.activeFact: That quick tapping now often means a downy woodpecker is staking out spring space.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Large woodland woodpecker growing louder with spring breeding activity.
critters.fact: Hairy woodpeckers become easier to hear in early April as drumming and calling continue to rise.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, hairy woodpeckers often work bigger trunks and advertise territory with sharp drumming.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Big forest woodpecker becoming more obvious as spring territory behavior ramps up.
critters.fact: Pileated woodpeckers are often louder in early spring as pairs maintain territories and move toward nesting.
critters.activeFact: A booming call or deep hammering burst now can signal a pileated woodpecker owning a section of woods.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Common woodland woodpecker more active and vocal in early spring.
critters.fact: Red-bellied woodpeckers often increase calling and drumming in early April while defending territory.
critters.activeFact: Early spring red-bellied woodpeckers may spend more time chasing rivals and less time quietly feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Gentle dove easing further into courtship and early nesting behavior.
critters.fact: Mourning doves often show more cooing and courtship flights in early spring as daylight keeps increasing.
critters.activeFact: By early April, mourning doves may already be paired up and working on simple nest spots.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Seasonal finch brightening up and shifting toward spring energy.
critters.fact: American goldfinches begin transitioning out of winter plumage in early spring as breeding season approaches.
critters.activeFact: Early April can bring livelier flock movement and the first stronger hints of bright male color.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-season songbird still common while spring continues pushing north.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed juncos remain active in early April, especially around feeders and exposed ground.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, juncos may still dominate the ground under feeders before later seasonal shifts.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Small social finch turning more vocal as spring nesting gets closer.
critters.fact: House finches often sing more in early April as pairs strengthen bonds and start defending nest areas.
critters.activeFact: Now they may shift from casual feeder use to more obvious pair behavior and song.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland finch tied to cone crops and lingering spring seed sources.
critters.fact: Purple finches may still move through in early spring, especially where woodland seeds remain plentiful.
critters.activeFact: Early April purple finch activity can come in short bursts, with quick feeding runs followed by quiet gaps.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Large ground bird shifting deeper into spring strut and display season.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys begin acting more focused on dominance and courtship as early spring progresses.
critters.activeFact: By early April, toms may be strutting more regularly as the breeding season gains steam.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest bird now entering a more active stretch of spring drumming season.
critters.fact: Ruffed grouse drum more often in early spring as males establish display sites and advertise to mates.
critters.activeFact: That distant engine-like thump in the woods gets more regular now as grouse really get going.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall wetland bird returning to reopened marshes and spring fields.
critters.fact: Sandhill cranes are one of the classic early spring returnees, often reappearing as wetlands reopen.
critters.activeFact: A sandhill crane call over wet ground in early April is one of spring’s loudest announcements.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Smart black bird active across roads, fields, and spring woodland edges.
critters.fact: American crows stay busy in early spring, pairing up and focusing more on nest territory.
critters.activeFact: In early April, crows often gather loudly at dawn and patrol openings for food and trouble.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Large intelligent corvid active over ridges, timber, and opening spring skies.
critters.fact: Common ravens are often already in breeding mode by early April, with pairs staying closely coordinated.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, ravens may be seen soaring, calling, and working together over forest openings.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Soaring raptor moving deeper into visible nest and territory behavior.
critters.fact: Red-tailed hawks often work on nests in early spring, carrying sticks and calling more often.
critters.activeFact: In early April, hawks may spend more time soaring and nest-tending than sitting quietly to hunt.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland hawk entering stronger spring territory and nest behavior.
critters.fact: Cooper’s hawks become more territorial in early spring as nesting season approaches in wooded areas and neighborhoods.
critters.activeFact: Now they may make sharp, aggressive flights while defending space around early nest sites.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Common duck back on open water and pairing up across spring wetlands.
critters.fact: Mallards become more visible in early spring as ponds, creeks, and flooded low spots fully open.
critters.activeFact: Early April often finds mallards paired up and feeding across fresh open water and flooded edges.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Colorful cavity-nesting duck returning with active early spring wetland movement.
critters.fact: Wood ducks often arrive early and begin checking cavities as soon as quiet open water becomes available.
critters.activeFact: In early April, wood ducks may show up on flooded timber, creek bends, and calm ponds.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Migratory goose active around open water, shorelines, and early nest zones.
critters.fact: Canada geese are highly active in early spring as they pair up, claim shoreline space, and prepare for nesting.
critters.activeFact: Early April can make geese extra noisy and territorial around ponds, marshes, and nearby fields.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Large wading bird returning as streams, marshes, and pond edges open for hunting.
critters.fact: Great blue herons often reappear in early spring as creeks and wetlands reopen enough for steady feeding.
critters.activeFact: A heron along an open creek or pond edge in early April is a classic quiet comeback scene.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Basking turtle starting to use sunny days more often as waters warm.
critters.fact: Painted turtles begin emerging more in early spring and use bright days to warm up on logs and banks.
critters.activeFact: A warm April afternoon can put painted turtles out basking even if nights still dip cold.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Large aquatic turtle slowly waking as shallow water temperatures climb.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles remain subdued in early spring but begin stirring as shallows gradually warm.
critters.activeFact: Early April may bring the first slow shift from winter stillness toward more snapping turtle movement.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Panfish reacting to longer days and slightly warming spring shallows.
critters.fact: Bluegill start moving more in early spring as daylight and mild warming pull them toward feeding zones.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, bluegill often hold near cover where warmer water and food begin gathering.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Freshwater predator easing closer to spring staging areas and warming cover.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass begin shifting toward shallower staging areas in early spring as edges slowly warm.
critters.activeFact: In early April, bass are often closer to cover near warming water but not fully shallow yet.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Schooling fish entering one of the earliest active windows of spring.
critters.fact: Yellow perch are among the early spawners and often become more active as spring water temperatures rise.
critters.activeFact: Early April perch may gather in schools near shallower water as spawning approaches or unfolds.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Fast predator moving through one of its prime early spring periods.
critters.fact: Northern pike are early spawners and often move into shallow, weedy, or flooded areas soon after ice-out.
critters.activeFact: This stage of spring is prime time for pike movement toward soft shallow habitat and spawning areas.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny frog ready to headline the earliest real chorus nights of spring.
critters.fact: Spring peepers are among the earliest calling frogs and often begin once warm spring nights reach wetland edges.
critters.activeFact: A mild early April evening can bring peeper calls that suddenly make a wetland sound fully alive.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Large pond frog still waiting for warmer water before getting truly active.
critters.fact: Bullfrogs usually stay quieter than the earliest spring frogs because they prefer warmer water and steadier conditions.
critters.activeFact: In early April, bullfrogs are often still lagging behind peepers and other first-voice amphibians.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Common shoreline frog beginning to stir as spring warmth builds along wet edges.
critters.fact: Green frogs usually lag behind the earliest callers but start responding as spring temperatures continue rising.
critters.activeFact: By early April, green frogs may begin shifting from winter hiding toward more shoreline movement.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted wetland frog among the earlier amphibians to move in spring.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs can become active in early spring warm spells, especially around shallow wetlands and marsh edges.
critters.activeFact: Early April may bring leopard frogs out near open wet ground, shallow pools, and sunny marsh margins.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Harmless snake often among the first reptiles spotted each spring.
critters.fact: Garter snakes are commonly one of the earliest snakes to emerge once spring sun begins warming exposed cover.
critters.activeFact: A sunny April afternoon can bring garter snakes out near rocks, boards, brush piles, and trail edges.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic snake waiting on steadier warmth along spring shorelines.
critters.fact: Water snakes begin stirring in spring as shorelines warm, though early activity still depends heavily on temperature.
critters.activeFact: During especially sunny early April days, water snakes may show limited movement near open water edges.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Woodland snake beginning to reappear as spring sunlight reaches shelter spots.
critters.fact: Eastern milk snakes start becoming active in early spring as boards, rocks, and old wood warm in the sun.
critters.activeFact: Early April can produce milk snake sightings around barns, woodpiles, and brushy debris on mild afternoons.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Migratory butterfly still mostly south of northern Michigan as spring opens further.
critters.fact: In early spring, monarch migration is underway farther south, but northern Michigan usually waits longer for broad arrivals.
critters.activeFact: Early April is more about monarchs advancing north than appearing widely around local fields and woods.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Large butterfly still waiting on steadier warmth and stronger nectar flow.
critters.fact: Swallowtail activity in northern Michigan usually picks up later than early April once warmth and blooms increase.
critters.activeFact: At this point in spring, swallowtails are still more of a promise than a regular sight.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Aerial predator still mostly developing underwater this early in the season.
critters.fact: Most dragonflies in early spring are still nymphs underwater, waiting for much warmer conditions to emerge.
critters.activeFact: Early April wetlands hold future dragonflies below the surface long before summer patrol flights begin.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Slender wetland insect still mostly hidden in aquatic stages during early spring.
critters.fact: Damselflies spend much of their life underwater as nymphs, and most are not yet flying in early April.
critters.activeFact: This early in the season, damselflies are more tucked into pond margins than hovering above them.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer glow insect still dormant while spring keeps building.
critters.fact: Fireflies are not active in early spring, spending this time in immature stages hidden in soil and leaf litter.
critters.activeFact: Early April is still far too soon for blinking firefly nights, even if the woods feel more awake.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Pollinator taking advantage of the first decent warm flights of spring.
critters.fact: Honeybees may fly on warm early April afternoons to cleanse, scout, and work the earliest blooms.
critters.activeFact: A sunny spring afternoon can bring honeybees buzzing out briefly even while mornings still start frosty.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Cold-tolerant bee among the first bold pollinators to reappear each spring.
critters.fact: Queen bumblebees are often among the earliest large bees seen in spring because they can handle cooler conditions.
critters.activeFact: Early April can bring lone queen bumblebees out searching for flowers and future nesting spots.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Jumping insect still mostly absent from the early spring scene up north.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers are generally not active yet in northern Michigan, with visible movement arriving much later in warmer months.
critters.activeFact: This part of spring is still quiet for grasshoppers, with the season only setting the table for later activity.
